section.title: Jerusalem's Refusal to Repent
section.sectionRationale: This unit presses the covenant lawsuit further by testing the city itself: can even one person of justice and truth be found, from the poor to the powerful. The universal failure justifies the coming disaster, while the closing scene exposes leaders and people alike as content with comfortable lies.
section.thesis: Not one person in Jerusalem, poor or powerful, can be found who acts justly and seeks truth, so the LORD brings a fearsome, ancient nation against a people who love false prophets and corrupt priests more than the truth.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the search for a single just person in Jerusalem's streets; the ancient, mighty nation summoned as instrument of judgment; the foolish, senseless people who neither see nor hear; and the wicked among the people who prosper by deceit while priests rule by their own authority.

units.label: Search for one just person
units.note: not one who seeks the truth

verses.text: “Go up and down the streets of Jerusalem. Look now and take note; search her squares. If you can find a single person, anyone who acts justly, anyone who seeks the {{emet|truth}}, then I will forgive the city.
Although they say, ‘As surely as the LORD lives,’ they are swearing falsely.”

O LORD, do not Your eyes look for truth? You struck them, but they felt no pain. You finished them off, but they refused to accept discipline. They have made their faces harder than stone and refused to repent.
Then I said, “They are only the poor; they have played the fool, for they do not know the way of the LORD, the justice of their God.

I will go to the powerful and speak to them. Surely they know the way of the LORD, the justice of their God.” 
But they too, with one accord, had broken the yoke and torn off the chains.

Therefore a lion from the forest will strike them down, a wolf from the desert will ravage them. A leopard will lie in wait near their cities, and everyone who ventures out will be torn to pieces. For their rebellious acts are many, and their unfaithful deeds are numerous.

“Why should I forgive you? Your children have forsaken Me 
and sworn by gods that are not gods. I satisfied their needs, yet they committed adultery and assembled at the houses of prostitutes.
They are well-fed, lusty stallions, each neighing after his neighbor's wife.
Should I not punish them for these things?” declares the LORD. “Should I not avenge Myself on such a nation as this?

units.label: An ancient nation summoned
units.note: an established nation whose language you do not know

verses.text: Go up through her vineyards and ravage them, but do not finish them off. Strip off her branches, for they do not belong to the LORD.
For the house of Israel and the house of Judah have been utterly unfaithful to Me,” declares the LORD.

They have lied about the LORD and said: 
“He will not do anything; harm will not come to us; we will not see sword or famine.

The prophets are but wind, for the word is not in them. 
So let their own predictions befall them.”

Therefore this is what the LORD God of Hosts says: 
“Because you have spoken this word, I will make My words a fire in your mouth 
and this people the wood it consumes.

Behold, I am bringing a distant nation against you, O house of Israel,” declares the LORD. “It is an established nation, an ancient nation, a nation whose language you do not know and whose speech you do not understand.
Their quivers are like open graves; they are all mighty men.

They will devour your harvest and food; they will consume your sons and daughters; they will eat up your flocks and herds; they will feed on your vines and fig trees. With the sword they will destroy the fortified cities in which you trust.”
“Yet even in those days,” declares the LORD, “I will not make a full end of you.

And when the people ask, ‘For what offense has the LORD our God done all these things to us?’ You are to tell them, ‘Just as you have forsaken Me and served foreign gods in your land, so will you serve foreigners in a land that is not your own.’”

units.label: Foolish and senseless
units.note: eyes that do not see, ears that do not hear

verses.text: Declare this in the house of Jacob and proclaim it in Judah:
“Hear this, O {{nabal|foolish}} and senseless people, who have eyes but do not see, who have ears but do not hear.

Do you not fear Me?” declares the LORD. “Do you not tremble before Me, the One who set the sand as the boundary for the sea, 
an enduring barrier it cannot cross? The waves surge, but they cannot prevail. They roar but cannot cross it.
But these people have stubborn and rebellious hearts. They have turned aside and gone away.
They have not said in their hearts, ‘Let us fear the LORD our God, who gives the rains, both autumn and spring, in season, who keeps for us the appointed weeks of harvest.’
Your iniquities have diverted these from you; your sins have deprived you of My bounty.

units.label: Wicked among my people
units.note: prophets prophesy falsely, priests rule by their own authority

verses.text:
For among My people are wicked men; they watch like fowlers lying in wait; 
they set a trap to catch men.

Like cages full of birds, so their houses are full of deceit. 
Therefore they have become powerful and rich.

They have grown fat and sleek, and have excelled in the deeds of the wicked. They have not taken up the cause of the fatherless, that they might prosper; nor have they defended the rights of the needy.
Should I not punish them for these things?” declares the LORD. “Should I not avenge Myself on such a nation as this?
A horrible and shocking thing has happened in the land.
The prophets prophesy falsely, and the priests rule by their own authority. My people love it so, but what will you do in the end?

emet.gloss: Truth, faithfulness, reliability. God's search of Jerusalem's streets is not for perfection but for even one person who genuinely seeks and lives by emet, showing how far a hollow, oath-swearing religiosity had drifted from real integrity before God.

nabal.gloss: Foolish, senseless, morally obtuse rather than merely intellectually slow. This is the same root used of the fool who says in his heart there is no God, Psalm 14:1, describing a willful blindness that has eyes and ears yet refuses to see or hear the obvious evidence of God.

basic.context: God sends Jeremiah on a search through Jerusalem's streets: can even one person be found who acts justly and seeks truth? From the poorest to the most powerful, the answer is no, and so an ancient, terrifying nation whose language no one understands is summoned as the LORD's chosen instrument of judgment. The chapter ends by exposing the whole religious system, prophets who lie and priests who rule by their own authority, propped up by a people who actually prefer it that way.
basic.hermeneutics: This is prophetic covenant lawsuit language building toward a specific historical judgment, the coming Babylonian invasion, framed as both deserved punishment and revelation of just how deep and universal Judah's corruption had become. The uncomfortable honesty here is verse 31, my people love it so, this is not a people deceived against their will but one that actively prefers comfortable lies to hard truth, and Scripture does not let that be softened.
basic.originalLanguages: God searches for anyone who seeks emet, truth or faithfulness, not abstract correctness but a reliable, trustworthy life lived before Him; none is found. By verse 21 the people are called nabal, foolish, the same word used of the fool who says in his heart there is no God, describing not low intelligence but willful spiritual blindness in those who have eyes and ears yet refuse to use them.
basic.typology: The vain search for a single righteous person in Jerusalem anticipates Paul's sweeping indictment that none is righteous, no, not one, Romans 3:10, a universal diagnosis this passage previews on a smaller, national scale. Only in Christ, the one truly righteous man, is the search this chapter fails to complete finally answered.
basic.christ: Where Jeremiah cannot find one just person in all of Jerusalem, the New Testament identifies the one man in whom perfect justice and truth are finally found, Christ, who alone fulfills what verse 1 searches for in vain and who alone can forgive not merely a spared city but a redeemed people. Paul's universal indictment in Romans 3:10-18 draws on exactly this kind of prophetic search coming up empty, resolved only in the righteousness given through faith in Jesus.
basic.god: God is patient and searching, willing to forgive the entire city for the sake of even one faithful person, yet unflinchingly honest about what that search reveals: total, willful unfaithfulness from the poorest to the most powerful. His judgment through a foreign, terrifying nation is severe, yet even here He limits it, I will not make a full end.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 18:22-33 has Abraham similarly plead for Sodom on the basis of finding even a few righteous people. Romans 3:10-18 gives Paul's universal verdict, none is righteous, echoing the failed search of this chapter on a global scale. Psalm 14:1-3 uses the same picture of fools who do not seek God. Habakkuk 1:5-11 similarly describes a fierce, foreign nation raised up as God's instrument of judgment.
basic.oneStory: This is the moment the covenant lawsuit becomes a citywide verdict: not one person can be found who lives by truth and justice, so judgment through a foreign power becomes necessary and just. That universal failure, previewed here in one city, is diagnosed for the whole human race in Romans, and answered only in Christ, the one righteous man whose life and death make forgiveness possible for all who trust Him.

context.literary: The chapter is structured as a series of divine speeches interspersed with Jeremiah's own voice, opening with a search motif (5:1-9), moving to direct address of the coming invading nation (5:10-19), then two further indictments of the people's senselessness (5:20-25) and their corrupt leadership (5:26-31), each closing with the refrain, should I not punish them for these things.
context.historical: This oracle likely dates from Jeremiah's earlier ministry, before Babylon's dominance was fully established, so the description of an ancient, established nation whose language is unknown functions as an ominous, still-unfolding warning rather than a report of an already-arrived army.
context.canonical: The failed search for a righteous person anticipates the New Testament's fuller doctrine of universal human sinfulness, particularly Paul's catena of Old Testament citations in Romans 3, making this chapter an important Old Testament witness to the biblical diagnosis that no one is righteous before God apart from grace.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To demonstrate through an exhaustive search, poor and powerful alike, that Judah's corruption is total and inexcusable, justifying God's decision to bring a foreign nation as an instrument of judgment, while still signaling that even this judgment has limits and is not the final word.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage describes a specific historical verdict on Jeremiah's Jerusalem and a specific instrument of judgment, the coming Babylonian invasion; it does not prescribe how every future judgment must be carried out. The enduring truth is that widespread, willful rejection of truth and justice invites real consequence, and that comfortable, popular lies are no substitute for genuine faithfulness.

debates.question: Does the search in 5:1 imply that finding even one righteous person could have averted judgment on the whole city, similar to Abraham's intercession for Sodom?

views.label: Yes, a real conditional offer of mercy
views.case: The parallel to Genesis 18, where God agrees to spare Sodom for the sake of a handful of righteous people, suggests this search is a genuine offer, corporate judgment could have been stayed by even minimal covenant faithfulness present in the city.

views.label: A rhetorical device exposing total corruption
views.case: Others read the search primarily as a rhetorical strategy to demonstrate, beyond doubt, just how comprehensive Judah's unfaithfulness was, its main function being diagnostic rather than a live legal condition that could have changed the outcome.

debates.bearing: Either way, the passage teaches that God's judgment here is not capricious; it follows a genuine, thorough search that found no faithful remnant sufficient to avert corporate consequence, underscoring both divine patience and inescapable justice.

study.originalLanguages: God's search is for emet, truth, a life of reliable faithfulness rather than mere ritual correctness, and it comes up empty from the poor to the powerful alike. By verse 21 the people are labeled nabal, foolish, the same term applied to the fool who denies God in his heart, describing willful refusal to see and hear rather than simple ignorance.

typology.type: The vain search for one righteous person in Jerusalem, Jeremiah 5:1
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates Paul's universal verdict that none is righteous, Romans 3:10-18, and its answer in Christ, the one truly righteous man.

study.christ: Where this chapter's search for even a single just and truthful person in Jerusalem comes up completely empty, the New Testament reveals that only one man ever fulfilled such a search perfectly, Christ, and identifies the same universal failure in every nation and every heart, none is righteous, no, not one, Romans 3:10. It is only through faith in this one righteous man that the forgiveness this chapter searches for in vain becomes possible for sinners everywhere.
study.god: God is patient enough to offer forgiveness for an entire corrupt city on the strength of even one faithful witness, yet He is unsparingly honest when that search fails completely. His judgment through a fearsome foreign power is real and severe, yet even in announcing it He commits not to make a full end, holding justice and mercy together without collapsing either into the other.

crossReferences.note: Abraham intercedes for Sodom on the basis of finding even a handful of righteous people, a similar pattern of a search that could avert judgment.
Paul's universal indictment, none is righteous, echoes and expands the failed search of this chapter to the whole human race.
Uses the same picture of fools who deny God and fail to seek Him, corresponding to Jeremiah's nabal, foolish people.
Describes a similarly fierce, foreign nation raised up by God as an instrument of judgment on His own unfaithful people.

study.oneStory: This is the moment the covenant lawsuit becomes a citywide verdict: not even one person can be found who lives by truth and justice, making judgment through a foreign nation both necessary and just. That failed search, small in scale here, is diagnosed for the whole human race in Romans and answered fully only in Christ, the one righteous man through whom forgiveness becomes possible for all who trust Him.

quiz.q: What does God ask Jeremiah to search for in Jerusalem's streets?

options.text: A single person who acts justly and seeks the truth, so the city could be forgiven.
options.note: Verse 1 frames this as a genuine search whose failure justifies the coming judgment.

options.text: The wealthiest family in the city.
options.note: The search is about moral and spiritual faithfulness, not wealth.

options.text: The most religious-looking worshippers at the temple.
options.note: The passage explicitly notes that oath-swearing and outward religion did not equal true faithfulness; verse 2 says they swear falsely even while invoking the LORD's name.

options.text: A prophet willing to speak only comforting words.
options.note: The search is for genuine justice and truth, and the chapter later condemns exactly this kind of comfortable false prophecy.

quiz.q: What is the outcome of the search among both the poor and the powerful?

options.text: Both groups alike are found to have broken the yoke and rejected God's ways.
options.note: Verses 4-5 show the search extending from the poor to the powerful, with the same unfaithful result in both cases.

options.text: The poor were found faithful while the powerful were corrupt.
options.note: The text shows both groups equally unfaithful, not one group faithful and the other corrupt.

options.text: The powerful were found faithful while the poor were corrupt.
options.note: This reverses the text; both groups are shown to be equally unfaithful.

options.text: The search was never actually completed.
options.note: The passage explicitly reports the search's outcome for both groups.

quiz.q: How is the coming invading nation described in verses 15-17?

options.text: An ancient, established nation whose language the people do not know or understand.
options.note: Verse 15 emphasizes both its antiquity and its utterly foreign, incomprehensible speech, heightening the sense of dread.

options.text: A small band of local raiders.
options.note: The passage describes a formidable, ancient nation, not a small local group.

options.text: A nation that speaks the same language as Judah.
options.note: The text explicitly says their language is unknown and not understood.

options.text: A peaceful trading partner seeking an alliance.
options.note: The nation is described as devouring harvests and destroying fortified cities, clearly a military threat, not a trading partner.

quiz.q: What does verse 31 reveal about the people's relationship to false prophecy and corrupt priesthood?

options.text: The people actually love having false prophets and self-serving priests.
options.note: The verse states plainly, my people love it so, showing active preference for comfortable lies, not mere passive deception.

options.text: The people were forced against their will to accept false teaching.
options.note: The text explicitly says the people love this arrangement, contradicting the idea of unwilling coercion.

options.text: The people had already rejected all false prophets by this point.
options.note: The verse describes ongoing false prophecy that the people actively love, not rejection of it.

options.text: Only the priests were corrupt while the prophets remained faithful.
options.note: The verse names both prophets and priests together as complicit in this corruption.

quiz.q: How does this chapter's failed search for a righteous person connect to the New Testament?

options.text: Paul's declaration that none is righteous, no, not one, echoes and universalizes this same failed search.
options.note: Romans 3:10-18 draws on this kind of Old Testament pattern to make a global claim about human sinfulness, answered only in Christ.

options.text: It has no meaningful connection to the New Testament.
options.note: Paul's universal indictment in Romans 3 draws directly on this kind of prophetic pattern.

options.text: It shows that some individuals can achieve perfect righteousness on their own.
options.note: The passage and its New Testament echo both emphasize the opposite, universal failure apart from grace.

options.text: It applies only to ancient Jerusalem and has no broader theological significance.
options.note: Its connection to Romans 3 gives it clear broader theological significance about universal sinfulness.

questions: God searched Jerusalem's streets for even one person who lived by truth and justice. If God searched your life the same way this week, what would He find?
Both the poor and the powerful in this chapter were found equally unfaithful. Where do you excuse your own compromises by comparing yourself to a different social group, rich or poor, than your own?
The people preferred comfortable lies from false prophets to hard truth, my people love it so. Where in your own life do you choose comfortable reassurance over hard, needed truth?
God says He will not make a full end even amid severe judgment. Where do you need to trust God's restraint and mercy in consequences you are currently facing?

This chapter's failed search for a righteous person points forward to Christ, the only truly righteous man. How does resting in His righteousness, rather than your own search for goodness, change how you face your own failures this week?

translationNotes.note: Go up through her vineyards uses agricultural imagery for the coming invasion, likening Judah to a vine being stripped rather than uprooted entirely, consistent with the chapter's repeated qualification that judgment, though severe, will not be total.

The image of sand as the sea's unbreakable boundary recalls Job 38:8-11 and Psalm 104:9, both using the same picture of God's fixed limits on chaos as evidence of His power that ought to inspire reverent fear.

Priests rule by their own authority translates language literally closer to at their hands, meaning the priests exercise power on their own terms rather than under God's authority, a picture of religious leadership co-opted for self-interest.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit this session.
